How would we avoid all 3 of the above problems? It’s not that difficult, but with a caveat that you didn’t already overcommit to bigger architectural mistakes. Still, it’s never too late to stop making things worse. So without further ado, here’s the code free of all of the above problems.

def retrieve_name user:    User.find(@id),
                  email:   user.email,
                  api:     @api,
                  profile: api.fetch_profile(email)
  profile.name
end

You might’ve just done a double-take: wait, how is this the solution? We just removed caching and added a bunch of useless arguments. Bear with me, let’s talk through this real quick.

First of all, the arguments in this solution are a bit extra granular, we could have omitted some, and that’s up to you to decide. Second of all, note that all arguments are optional, so the method can still be called without passing anything. Finally, let’s go back and see if we’ve addressed the problems with the original code.

1. Is caller still misled about the real impact of calling this?

No. The fact that this method name is now a verb retrieve_user makes it clear that when you call it, it will do things. That’s all it takes to send the correct signal.

2. Can the caller control cache invalidation?

Yes.

name = retrieve_name

# Name is now cached, feel free to reuse it.
do_something_with(name)
do_something_else_with(name)

# Get a fresh name whenever you want.
fresh_name = retrieve_name

3. Can the caller stop redundant work from happening?

Totally.

my_user = User.find(123)
name = retrieve_name(user: my_user) # Saves a database call.

profile = @api.fetch_profile('foo@example.com')
name = retrieve_name(user: nil, email: nil, profile: profile) # Saves a db and a network call.

# P.S. But in the latter case you might as well just call profile.name instead.

In case it’s not obvious, we couldn’t accept arguments the same way in the original version, because we’re only caching one value, and even if we then passed a different user, we would still get back the first cached value.

Ultimately, with very little effort, we just gained 3 significant advantages in maintainability, reusability, and performance of our code.

FAQ

What if I need to call this method from different places, so I don’t have a variable to reuse?

I feel your pain. Unfortunately, if you must depend on this caching technique because you cannot assign a variable once, and pass it around, I have some bad news for you. Your abstractions need rethinking. There should be a top level routine in your code that tells the story of a particular transaction. Values that are reused need to be floated up into that context and passed into whatever needs them. In a vanilla Rails world the place like this would be your controller actions. If doing this makes your actions too long, you’re missing intermediary objects that give you a clean abstraction to write your routine. That said, this is a pretty big topic best left for future blog posts.

item.title: Kodable is hiring a senior game developer to help teach millions of kids to code
item.description:
Introducing Easy Apply
We've partnered with select companies to enable developers to apply to jobs on Stack Overflow. Use your Developer Story for a faster application.
Posted 15 days ago

Job description

We're looking for someone to ready to lead the development of a profitable, growing company (we doubled our revenue last year) that teaches tens of millions of kids to code. You'll work directly with top-tier artists, game designers, and educators from Harvard, Leapfrog, Teach For America, and more to build games that teach computer science to kids while *actually* being fun! The best part: this is a hands-on role. Less meetings, more coding! Sound exciting? Read on...

About the Role

You’ll direct development of all game-related features, with the opportunity to work in a variety of areas including game frameworks, game design, UI, networking, optimization and everything in between. This is a direct-producer role, we're looking for someone that wants to build the product, not just direct.

Kodable is multi-platform with a main codebase written in JavaScript using Cocos2d-X. We have production deployments for Web and iOS that routinely get hundreds of thousands of users a week. As we grow, you'll also be directly responsible for leading, growing, and building your own team to work with. 

About the team:

The Kodable team is full of bright, ambitious people who are passionate about teaching kids to code. You’ll work with a product team with over 50 years of collective experience creating top-notch games for companies like EA, Leapfrog, and Scientific Learning. Every member of the team has a voice in making Kodable great.

You'll join a remote-first team that prioritizes communication, collaboration, and insights from diverse backgrounds when building new features. Most of the team is based in the Bay Area, but includes team members from across the United States as well as immigrants from Kenya and Kyrgyzstan. Kodable places value on creating an inclusive culture, where we can learn from each other’s different experiences.

We also focus on making each other better as people, not just as workers. For example, we have a #pushups channel in Slack (complete with pushup-coach bot) and we all set a weekly goal for ourselves that has nothing to do with work. Work/Life Harmony is a big focus at Kodable, and we prioritize our own mental health instead of just the typical startup grind.

At Kodable, you’ll have the opportunity to grow as a person, as well as professionally. Join the #pushups Slack channel (complete with pushup-coach bot) if you want to. You’ll also set a personal goal for yourself each week, that has nothing to do with work. Work/Life Harmony is important at Kodable, and we prioritize our own mental health instead of just the typical startup grind.

Who we’re looking for:

  • You care deeply about programming education
  • You have 3+ years of JavaScript development experience in a production environment 
  • You have 3+ years experience in game development using frameworks such as Cocos2d, Unreal, Unity, XNA, LibGDX, Pygame, etc
  • You have released, or been a significant part of the public release of a game (doesn't need to be anything major like a Nintendo game, but ideally something that got a few users)

Highly Preferable (but not required) if:

  • You have experience with any Cocos2d frameworks (Cocos2d-X, JS, iPhone). If you don't - that's ok! We can help you get up to speed
  • You have experience with networking and interfacing with server-side code (saving/loading data, offline syncing, etc)
  • You have experience building and deploying multi-platform games (deploying to Web and iOS, for example)

Bonus Points if:

  • You have experience with REST APIs and JavaScript frameworks such as Angular, Vue, or React
  • You have experience working with a JavaScript game framework in a production environment, or experience with a game framework such as Unity and accompanying experience with JavaScript in a production environment.
  • You know COBOL and are an expert at punch-card coding 

About Kodable

Programming for Kids

At Kodable, we teach kids how to code before they can read. In the past 6 years, we've helped kids in over 50% of US Elementary Schools and 190+ countries around the world learn the fundamentals of computer science.

Kodable is designed to touch every part of a child’s life - from their home to their classroom. We’ve developed a complete, scaffolded curriculum that teachers, progress and sharing tools for parents, and, most importantly, a game that kids love. Filled with fuzzy characters that kids absolutely adore, we’ve created an entire universe that not only draws kids in, but makes them eager to explore.

We believe in the power of computer science to change the world. Never before has the ability to create so much value needed so little resources. All anyone needs is a computer and a dream. At Kodable, we set kids on the path to achieve that dream.

Benefits

  • Help teach millions of kids how to code
  • Work with a team from Google, Harvard, and more!
  • Flexible work-from-home schedule
  • 100% of Health, Vision, Dental, and Life Insurance coverage
  • Gym Stipend - We'll cover the cost of your gym membership!
  • Free Team Lunches

Joel Test

Source control
One-step build
Daily builds
Bug database
Bugs fixed before writing new code
Up-to-date schedule
Specs
Quiet working conditions
Best tools that money can buy
Testers
Code screening
Hallway usability testing

item.title: Why is Japan still a coronavirus outlier?
item.description:
At the time of writing, Japan has just over 900 confirmed cases of coronavirus. That’s 900 cases recorded over a two-month period since the first person — a man who had traveled to Wuhan — was confirmed to have the disease while in a Japanese hospital between Jan. 10 and 15.

In Italy, the first case was recorded two weeks later than in Japan, on Jan. 23. Shortly after, 50,000 people were quarantined in a handful of towns in the Lombardy region. Then, it was the entire north. Now, the entire country is in quarantine, with over 40,000 confirmed cases and 3,600 dead.

In the U.S., New York has closed its bars and restaurants and California has imposed similarly wide-scale “shelter in place” restrictions on the movement of its people and the activities of its citizens.

Look around Tokyo now, and you’ll see no such scene has unfolded. Despite the cancellation of sports events, the closure of schools, and the shutting down of some, but not all, larger entertainment venues, much of Japan continues as normal. There is no quarantine and no enforced closures of bars or restaurants. Even clubs (easy places to get sick at the best of times) remain open.

If you want to eat ramen at 4 a.m., fine. If you get on the subway, you’ll see it slightly emptier, but still heaving. If you want to rent a car and drive from one end of the country and back again, do it — there’s nothing stopping you. Which means there’s very little stopping the spread of the disease either.

So why the difference? How has Japan seemingly escaped the spread of the coronavirus when other countries have suffered so badly?

When you look at the factors that have made the virus spread quickly and easily elsewhere, Japan meets all of the same criteria. It has close contact with China, where the disease originated. Its cities are vast and dense, people are crammed into small apartments and squeezed together on trains (more so than many other cities), and despite requests for people to stagger their commutes and attempt to telework, Tokyo, with its population of 38 million people, remains busy.

One reason that Italy has been hit so badly, according to the World Health Organization, is the age of its population: The country has the oldest average age in Europe, the second oldest in the world, and coronavirus disproportionately affects the elderly, whose immune systems may not be strong enough to stave off the pneumonia the virus causes. Here, Japan one-ups Italy, its population is the world’s oldest and is just as vulnerable.

A common factor behind the deaths recorded in China was smoking — those with already damaged lungs are more likely to succumb pneumonia. Here again, Japan triumphs in its vulnerability — the government still owns 33 percent of Japan Tobacco and legislation toward smoking here is extremely lax. In 2017, Japan had the highest rate of male smoking among the G7 nations.

And it’s not like Japan has placed strict measures on its citizens to keep the disease under control. It has neither imposed the level of quarantine we saw in China to curb the outbreak, nor has it been strict with its travel restrictions. Most travelers can still visit Japan, and those from restricted countries aren’t banned entirely, they are just asked to voluntarily self-isolate for 14 days.

So again you have to ask why the difference? How is Japan reporting such low numbers?

The cynical answer points to the country’s low testing rates, that Japan has recorded such a low number of cases precisely because it isn’t testing. Of course, a question mark hangs over the Olympics and whether the government is attempting to keep confirmed cases low so that the games proceed as planned.

Unlike South Korea, where we are seeing rigorous testing, and despite World Health Organization advice to “test, test, test,” Japan has stuck to a policy of testing only those with extended visible symptoms or a history of direct contact with those who have tested positive, attempting to isolate small clusters before they grow. New tests that produce produce results in 10-15 minutes are becoming available, but even with the improved tech, under the current policy a test will only be administered in the most extreme circumstances, when people have had a fever for four days or more.

But if the government’s testing regime is a failure, surely we’d see evidence of the disease’s spread in other ways. Its presence would appear not as positive test results but in the guise of an overwhelmed health care system and overcrowded mortuaries. We have seen no such evidence.

Three trains of thought lead from here: conspiracy, good fortune and efficacy.

Conspiracy would suggest that there is a widespread cover up, that people are dying in their homes, untested and untreated, or being given false death records in hospital. It is, however, hard to believe that a nation’s worth of doctors would be, or could be, silent if the number of deaths we are seeing in Italy were occurring here. While an authoritarian government might dream of being able to control its population to that extent, the reality is unattainable; doctors would speak out to prevent deaths — we saw them speaking out in China, we’d see it here.

It is equally hard to get onboard the second train of thought: that Japan is simply fortunate. That the disease here just hasn’t spread in the way it has elsewhere due to a number of pre-existing conditions: relatively less social intimacy (bowing vs. shaking hands), an inclination to wear masks when sick that has existed since long before this coronavirus, already high rates of isolation amongst the elderly, and what little voluntary self-isolation and social distancing there is has meant that Japan is flattening its curve without a truly active attempt to.

Then there is the third option, that Japan’s “just enough” efforts, built upon those pre-existing conditions have simply worked. That targeted testing where needed has contained the disease where it has emerged; that early closure of mass events did do enough to prevent widespread contact of the infected with the healthy; and that decentralized efforts stemming from individuals and corporations (zero contact pizzas, everyone) have halted its spread without heavy-handed government directive. But “just enough” feels like an awfully precarious position to be in and without the coordinated efforts that have forced other populations to limit the spread of the virus, you have to ask: How long will the situation last?

The “critical” two-to-three week containment period proclaimed by Prime Minister Shinzo Abe at the end of February has now come to an end and some schools are set to reopen. If you head out to places like Tokyo’s Shinjuku Station or Shibuya Crossing, you’ll see them packed.

We’re now enjoying a three-day weekend, and it is the start of hanami (cherry blossoms viewing) season, which is typically marked by large picnics and parties beneath the trees. Already Shinjuku Gyoen, one of the capital’s largest parks, is busy with people taking pictures and swarming close between the nascent blossoms.

While official hanami events have been banned, the practice is so ingrained in the culture that it is hard to imagine people showing such restraint as to cancel picnics in their entirety.

If Japan’s not yet seen the worst of the disease, and studies suggest that it is yet to, will this be the catalyst?

item.title: It's time to start mourning the loss of 2020 so that we may survive it
item.description:
For most Americans, this has been a week of awakening and recognition, and hopefully, social distancing. What initially was met with skepticism and exceptionalism by broad swaths of the country was brought into sharp relief with NBA player Rudy Gobert’s diagnosis (along with Tom Hanks) and the cascade of cancellations that quickly followed. But now, as the US confirms over 25,000 cases, that awakening is giving way to a deep-seated suspicion that will soon become confirmed: the affects of this pandemic are here to stay, and likely to stay for the duration.

Imperial College of London’s epidemiological team released a sobering report that modeled what many of us have been fearing: these measures may need to go on considerably longer than 15 days for us to have any chance against the virus. The federal government followed suit, estimating that the pandemic could last 18 months.

That’s it for 2020. This is our plague year. This is our lost year. At this point, the strategy is one of containment. Not of the virus: it’s too late for that. But instead, our new focus must be for the brunt of the economic, social, and physical devastation of the virus to be limited to this calendar year, to the best of our ability.

We can do this by aggressively pursuing social distancing and other “suppression” methods to slow the spread of the disease and buy us as much time as possible, now, and nationwide. While ICL’s report suggested 5 months of measures may be necessary, there’s a lot of disagreement with that sentiment, especially if people follow these protocols immediately, together.

I would be lying if I said this sort of news hadn’t hit me personally very hard. It feels almost impossible, like some sort of twisted fairy tale or horror novel where we’re all the protagonists, and are suddenly transported to a world where social interaction is poison, everyone is at risk, and the economy collapses right before our very eyes. But it’s looking more and more each day like that’s the world we suddenly live in, together. We’re in the first 20 minutes of a disaster movie, and I don’t like it. (That movie? Contagion.)

It’s time to start mourning the loss of the world we knew, so that we can shift our focus to surviving this new world. This is not an easy process. We’ll all find ourselves lurching between the various stages of grief as we come to terms with the idea that the sports leagues are almost certainly not going to play again this year, that our friends and family are losing their jobs and worried, that we can’t enjoy our social connections the same way, that our favorite restaurants are in serious peril and many may not come back, and most difficult and frightening of all, that friends and loved ones are going to get infected, and some of them will die.

Most of us are in the “denial” phase of processing this grief: the sort of reality we’re talking about, we just haven’t come to terms with.

How could life as we know it change so suddenly? Isn’t there still a chance things go back to normal soon? Myself, I’ve been lurching between denial, anger, bargaining, and depression, all at once. I’m not quite to acceptance yet. And to a degree, I’m still holding out hope. Hope that the measures we’re taking have a significant impact and help us get a handle on this. Hope that summer still gives some respite and a chance to prepare for a second wave in winter. Hope that a treatment comes through that helps significantly reduce the severity of this disease and make it manageable. An age-old anti-malarial called HCQ, coupled with a strong antibiotic called azithromycin (you know it as a Z-pak) had some very promising results in an extremely limited study, but we’re also being cautioned not to get our hopes up too much and that’s a good mentality. (Trump has taken this and run with it as a if it’s a golden goose, but there is still a considerable amount of research and validation before we just start going ham with this approach.)

I want to make it clear that I’m not suggesting we’ll never get back to normal. We’ll find a way forward and find a way to fight this and beat it. But my reason for writing this is so that we start to come to terms with just how long these measures are likely to last, and the sort of damage they’ll likely wreak on the economy along the way. The prevailing wisdom now is that we should expect some measure of social distancing for at least two months. Because everything we do is basically on a two week lag, we’re about to see a spike in cases from before our measures went into effect. We’re collectively hopeful that our healthcare systems can weather this first wave, and that we can add capacity and supplies quickly, but it’s already looking to be a challenge in New York and other cities are going to struggle soon too. Some cities will be hit sooner rather than later, and some won’t be hit as hard. America has a lot of diversity in its construction of cities, density, and temperature, and all of these things may cause the disease to spread differently throughout.

A quick aside about our healthcare workers: they are about to go to war. They are about to go to this war underfunded and criminally under-prepared, in terms of equipment. They are talented, hard-working people who will do everything they can to fight this battle for us on the front lines and keep as many of us alive as possible, but if you know a healthcare worker (and I mean anyone that works at a hospital, be it an MD, NP, RN, CNA, PCT, MSA, RT, Food Service, Housekeeping, Transpo, or anyone else I may have missed), start right now, working with them to find out how you can preemptively support them. Make sure they have food, toilet paper, supplies to live their lives at home. Make sure they have childcare. Ask them if there are any errands you can run for them. Keep your physical distance, but be there to support them. Check in on them, listen to them, be there for them. This is likely to be the hardest weeks of their lives for them and we need them now more than ever.

The Washington Post has this fantastic article on how long the social distancing will have to last. But the salient takeaway is: almost certainly longer than 15 days. So let’s start to process that, together, and figure out how we can all support each other during this time. The economic impact of this is going to be unlike anything we’ve ever seen, even with the steps the government is taking to try to prop things up. But we’ll find out a new normal and a way to emerge on the other side of this together, because that’s what we do. And if I’m wrong about this taking longer than two weeks, and the virus disappears and everyone is back to normal, I’ll be there celebrating with all of you.

Some tips on processing this and working and living through these times together:

  • Talk. Talk a lot. Talk to whomever you’re quarantined with, but also use Facetime, Duo, or What’s App to stay in touch with your loved ones, and especially make sure you’re using video chat. This sort of connection is so vital, make sure you’re making time to do this regularly with your friends and family. Don’t let this slip.
  • It’s okay to cry. It’s okay to grieve the loss of your plans, your goals, and your aspirations. It may not feel like much when people are dying from the disease, but it’s okay to be upset that this year is not going how we all planned, and we should allow ourselves that mental space to be supremely annoyed and upset about those things vanishing.
  • Practice self-care. Consider reading a book or watching some feel-good movies or TV. Stay informed, because that’s important, but also unplug from the relentless corona-stories.
  • If you were working at an office and now you’re full-time work from home, consider adding social video events throughout the day. We have a daily Quiplash game/video call that lets people stay connected with each other and not just about work.
  • Take a deep breath. It’ll help.
  • Plan some self-improvement exercises that fit well with staying inside and isolated. There are lots of online free courses, lots of YouTube tutorials, lots of time to write or create or share something silly. Make sure you stay creative and stay sharing. We’re all in this together.
  • Check in on your friends. Help others however you can. Be there for each other.
  • Read Scott Kelly’s wonderful list of tips on how to stay sane while in isolation. Dude spent a year in space, so he knows a thing or two about being trapped in one place.
  • Stay positive. I know how counter-productive this post is to that effort, but we will get through this.

I’m also going to take a moment to point out how infuriating the federal government’s response has been to this at literally every possible opportunity. This is what happens when you elect proud incompetence. Our own intelligence flagged this as a serious concern and high risk of pandemic back in January when the news first started to break out of China. While we were quick to shut down travel from China, which bought us a month, we then proceeded to squander that month, not prioritize aggressive testing, and not work to marshal the resources our healthcare workers are now finding themselves sorely lacking. And so now the wave hits.

Just this week, the administration insisted that they shouldn’t be a “shipping clerk” for these supplies and that governors should procure them themselves. This is lunacy. The federal government has war production powers and other means of coordination and management that could help facilitate these concerns, if there was coherent leadership in place. (Fortunately, there’s been some reversal on this stance, but no enforcement of the Defense Production Act yet.)

And so we’re seeing a patchwork of responses from the different states. As of this writing, New York, Illinois, Washington, and California have all ordered non-essential activities be paused or stopped. Arizona has made the move to stop dine-in options and close bars and gyms in counties with confirmed cases. Other states are following suit, slowly. This is fundamentally a soft lockdown. Italy is in the throes of a hard lockdown: you are not able to leave your house there without a specific, allowable purpose. (Even taking walks is banned there.) But we still have many states that have not yet gotten the memo that this is coming for them, and that the time for aggressive action is now. No more dine-in restaurants. No more gatherings. No more church, gyms, concerts, bars, movies. Those have to be off the table, for at least a few weeks, and across the board.

The virus spreads silently, and the typical person contracts the virus and can spread it for up to 5–7 days before they become symptomatic. That’s its secret weapon: we can’t easily identify who among us are spreading the virus. Even worse, as many as 20%-50% of people who have the virus will never develop symptoms, but will continue to spread it. This is why participating the social distancing measures we’re talking about is of such vital importance.

Now allow me to play President for a day with my list of things that should have been done a week or two (or more!) ago. Some are in the works, others are, mind-bogglingly, not yet ordered:

  • Set a standard of a nationwide lockdown to begin immediately, and hold the states to it. Offer resources to support it. This is our best chance to keep the damage mitigated here, and to make such measures last only weeks instead of literal months.
  • Trigger the Defense Production Act to force companies to produce additional ventilators and, to the extent we can, N95 masks. (The masks use “melt-blown fabric” as its filtering medium, which is difficult to manufacture and for which most of the production capacity exists… you guessed it… in China.)
  • Declare immediate federal emergencies in each of the states with more than 100 cases, freeing up money and resources to the states.
  • Help share childcare resource plans for healthcare workers. They are our frontline soldiers right now and they’re going to need all of our support.
  • Encourage ALL states to immediately begin siting and construction of field hospitals/quarantine wards. Seattle is doing this in a soccer field. NYC has identified four sites and they’re going to begin soon. Other states with relatively few cases should be standing up this capacity NOW. Better to convert an empty gymnasium now and not need it than find out that it’s too late. Make the Army Corps of Engineers available in their full capacity to help with the execution of these plans if needed.
  • Similarly, coordinate with regional public health and hospital authorities to put best practice into place for quarantine/recovery ward scaling. Some hospitals are doing this by converting entire floors, or in some cases, entire facilities into Covid-19 wards. This is important because you can gain economies of scale in terms of your workforce and your equipment by creating an assembly line of sorts. This is what China did with their Covid-specific “hospitals” in Wuhan. They had essentially long tunnels of several hundreds patients, all with Covid, and so they could systematically work their way down their patients and manage them all without having to cycle PPE or scrub in and out. This sort of assembly line thinking will be crucial.
  • Implement a coordinated resource requisition and fulfillment service, allowing states to filter requests for PPE (personal protective equipment) for healthcare workers through a federal response, that would allow us to ship and manage through our national stockpile, while also coordinating the resources being newly manufactured. Everything in our power should be done to ensure hospital workers have the PPE they need, but the need is so vast. If our healthcare workers are becoming sick or even just quarantined, we’re going to be in an even worse spot, quickly. Some hospitals have seen themselves go through 6 months of supplies in 7 days, because frankly, hospitals don’t usually work with a surge of this many highly infectious disease patients all at once. There simply aren’t that many cases of tuberculosis or measles, so the individual facility stockpiles don’t measure up. Also, typical practice is one mask per patient, which is part of why rethinking that approach with quarantine wards will be necessary.
  • Solicit all industrial partners to donate their supplies immediately. This is fortunately happening in many states: contractor grade N95s work for the healthcare environment, and a lot of companies have those on hand. If YOU have N95s on hand, donate them to your nearest hospital. (These are used at home in painting and carpentry projects, for example.) Every single one helps. Do it now.
  • Testing, testing, and far more testing. Reduce every last barrier to testing and establish a comprehensive surveillance system like we use for the flu. There are some really promising new tests coming out, including a 45-minute test that could be a game-changer. Put whatever resources into validating and scaling that concept up as possible. Then…
  • Encourage even asymptomatic people to get tested, by having so many tests available that this won’t take away from people in need. The more we know about the landscape of where the infections are, the faster we can pounce on hotspots and move resources around. We’re. Flying. Blind. The Imperial College study called out the importance of this strategy as well, which they referred to as “suppression”, as opposed to simple “mitigation.” The Atlantic has a fantastic piece that plots a path forward that doesn’t require all of us to stay home for 18 months. This very long Medium piece also plots a path where, perhaps, just a few weeks of this is enough.
  • We also need to fast-track work on serology tests, which we can use to determine the presence of antibodies. (Or, essentially, to let you know if you’ve been exposed to the virus, after the fact.) This will be a crucial feature of how we get back to normal society. While we don’t quite know the long-term immunity, we’re hoping it’s somewhat substantial, and knowing you’re immune would be a game changer.
  • Encourage all states to push an extremely aggressive temperature policy for essential workers. If you’re still going to work at, say, a utility or airport or courier service or restaurant, all workers should be temped at the start of their shift. If you’re over 99˚F, you’re sent home on paid sick leave. While infected persons are still able to transmit while completely asymptomatic, this would still weed out the more virulent people and lower our risk significantly.
  • I think it would also make sense to temp customers coming in to shop at grocery stores, as well. This can be done with no-contact infrared thermometers, and since the National Guard is being deployed here in Arizona at groceries, they could easily facilitate this check.

I know how bleak this all sounds. And that’s because it is bleak. We’re living in the time of the plague and we didn’t account for this. This was supposed to be our year. But we need to collectively focus our efforts on staying positive, encouraging each other, supporting each other, and doing what we can to keep the damage this goddamn virus will incur contained to this year, if at all possible.

I will say this: there are a few narrow paths forward that may emerge that make a return to normalcy more of a couple of month thing than an 18 month thing. A strong, proven treatment method could be huge. I’m really holding out hope for the HCQ/Z-pak combo or for the experimental drug remdesivir, but it’s just too early to say right now. If we have a highly effective protocol for this, then it becomes “go home, take these pills, self-isolate, see you in two weeks,” as opposed to “this is the worst pneumonia I’ve ever seen before.”

But while this virus is mild for many (most, even), it is extraordinarily dangerous for a small percentage of us. Even those of us under 40. Don’t let yourself be lulled into complacency because of the low mortality rate you’ve heard bandied about for people under 60. Even though it’s a small percentage of a whole, when the whole is huge, the raw numbers are scary. And the accounts of how this virus progresses, and how hard it can be to treat when it suddenly triggers Advanced Respiratory Distress Syndrome and Multiple Organ Dysfunction Syndrome… well, they’re terrifying.

The New York Times asked a number of epidemiologists to give their best and worst case scenarios, along with their reasoning. There’s quite a spectrum, but the worst case is… bad: 2 million people dying in the US, and the disease settling in to stay. I am hoping beyond hope that our outcome is one of the more rosy ones outlined here, but we also control the narrative with our own actions in these next crucial few weeks.
